title: Solvent-free processing of lithium-ion batteries via plasma treatment of electrodes for adhesive interfaces
abstract_summary: This study introduces a solvent-free, plasma-assisted dry-processing method for fabricating lithium-ion battery electrodes. It resolves the poor adhesion issues of polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) binders to current collectors by applying a short-duration argon plasma treatment to the dry-rolled electrode sheets before hot-pressing. This process completely eliminates the need for toxic solvents like N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) and intensive drying steps, while maintaining or improving electrochemical performance across LCO, LFP, and NMC chemistries.
green_strategy: Solvent-free dry-electrode manufacturing that eliminates toxic organic solvents (NMP) and high-energy thermal drying processes, utilizing dry-powder mixing, rolling, and dry plasma surface activation.
fabrication_method: Dry mixing of active materials, carbon black, pore former (adamantane), and PTFE binder; rolling into a freestanding sheet; vacuum sublimation of adamantane; surface Ar plasma treatment (450 V DC, 3-4 mA, 30 s); and hot pressing (150 C, 1.6 MPa, 1 min) onto Al current collector.

performance_metrics.condition: 30 s argon plasma-treated PTFE/LCO cathode sheet, hot-pressed at 150 C, 1.6 MPa
performance_metrics.comparison_baseline: could not be measured because it peeled off immediately, without adhesion

performance_metrics.condition: Full cell configuration cycled at 1.0C rate
Initial cycle at 1C (137 mA/g) using plasma-treated LCO cathode
DC voltage of 450 V, argon gas pressure 3.0 x 10^1 Pa

limitations: The dry formulation contains approximately 80 % active material, which is lower than the highest loadings achievable with some state-of-the-art dry processes.
Requires a vacuum chamber environment for the plasma treatment step in this proof-of-concept setup, which limits continuous high-throughput roll-to-roll processing.
Relies on the use of adamantane as a pore-former, which requires an additional thermal vacuum sublimation step (<10 Pa, 120 C) to introduce porosity.
Relatively low initial Coulombic efficiency (~76.8% at 1C) attributed to irreversible SEI formation and initial activation processes.

figure_or_table_evidence.evidence_summary: Displays (a) the stable adhesion strength of ~2.8 N/cm over 5 min, 1 day, and 1 week of exposure, (b) XPS O1s spectra demonstrating oxygen-containing functional groups after plasma treatment, and (c) water contact angle decreasing from 108 degrees (untreated) to 74-76 degrees after plasma treatment.

Compares electrochemical performance of LCO cathodes. Fig. 4(b) shows full-cell cycling performance at 1C over 300 cycles, showing improved discharge capacity and capacity retention for the plasma-treated cathode. Fig. 4(c) illustrates rate capability up to 5C, displaying higher capacity retention at high C-rates for plasma-treated electrodes.

candidate_research_claims.claim_text: Argon plasma surface treatment of dry-rolled PTFE-containing electrode sheets successfully creates oxygen-containing functional groups on the PTFE surface, increasing its adhesion strength to aluminum foil to ~2.8 N/cm and enabling stable dry-processed cathode operation.
candidate_research_claims.experimental_condition: 30 s Ar plasma treatment (450 V DC, 3-4 mA), hot pressed at 150 C and 1.6 MPa
candidate_research_claims.limitation: The processing current was limited to 3-4 mA, and the setup operates under vacuum conditions.
candidate_research_claims.citation_text: as shown in Fig. 3(a), the adhesive strength between the plasma-treated PTFE and aluminum foil was ~2.8 N/cm, which was confirmed to be sufficient and equivalent to that between the plasma-treated PTFE and other materials reported in previous studies

human_review_reason: Specific rate capability values in Fig. 4(c) are plotted in a chart and cannot be read with 100% exact numerical precision from the text, requiring manual digitization.
